paper_map.title: Evolution of microstructure, texture and mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C low-density steel during cold rolling
paper_map.abstract_summary: This study systematically investigated the evolution of microstructure and texture as well as mechanical properties during cold rolling of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C (wt%) steel. The stacking fault energy was estimated as ~108 mJ/m2. With cold rolling, yield strength exceeded 2.0 GPa after 90% reduction due to microstructure refinement, and various deformation substructures including Taylor lattice, microbands, nano-twins, shear bands and dislocation cells formed. The rolling texture exhibited a transition from 'copper-type' to 'brass-type' beyond 50% reduction, and deformation twinning occurred despite the high SFE, likely attributed to short-range order and local stress concentration.
paper_map.research_objective: To systematically investigate the microstructure and texture evolution as well as the mechanical properties of Fe–30Mn–11Al–1.2C (wt%) low-density steel during cold rolling up to 90% thickness reduction.
main_process_variables: Uniaxial tensile test (room temperature, initial strain rate 10^-3 s^-1)
key_sections: Introduction: Background on high Mn-Al-C steels, deformation mechanisms, and research objectives.
Material and experimental procedure: Fabrication, cold rolling, and characterization techniques (OM, EBSD, TEM, tensile testing).
Results: Mechanical properties, microstructure evolution (substructures), texture evolution (fibers and components).
Discussion: Microstructure–property relationships, dislocation glide modes, planar slip, twinning, and texture transition mechanisms (copper-type to brass-type).
Conclusions: Summary of key findings on cold rolling effects.
